inline_comments.message: I think Component anno is redundant here.
it is used only in constructor, it is not necessary to be a field
the same as above
I think that it would be safer to change this method to private static. When you call non-static method in constructor, it is possible to use the class field that has not been initialised yet.
this is quite dangerous, you call method in constructor that uses non-static class field (server).
There are no tests for this class
Those fields would be private if they were set in constructor
This class is dependent on two more specific classes. That's the violation of dependency invection principle.
I' not sure if I'm correct, but I don't like starting simulatior in init method. You can't have this class as a bean without running simulator every time

Those properties should be injected. Right now this class does two things - reads property file and as a controller. So it has two reasons to change - that is a violation of single responsibility principle.
Again, violation of dependency inversion principle.
this method can be static. It's bad practise to call non-static methods from constructor.

This lambda isn't easy to understand. I thought that you're checking if path leads to file, but it actually checks if file is a file or doesn't exist.

My suggestion would be either to implement that literally:
    file.toFile().isFile() || !file.toFile.exists()

or just create a functions with name isFileOrDoesntExists()

There can be whitespaces before array starts ;)

Add test to cover that case.
I would change the name of the function to actaully represent what it does.
license header missing

- make logger static final, 
- also rethink the uppercase variable name - is the LOGGER really a constant? is it fully immutable once created ? If not, then change it to lowercase. a constant is more than just 'static final' keywords when it comes to object references. The object itself has to be immutable.

Fully agree, this is a clear sign that no unit tests exist:) Dependency injection can be easily added considering you can use spring to wire the dependencies (of course without breaking encapsulation and testability)
A good practice is to use OS-independent path separator logic, instead of string concatenation.

technically this is not a properly defined rest resource, rather a method type url:) I'd suggest renaming it to a resource: /registration. The methods you can invoke on it can be easily represented with http methods :)
Suggestion: use Spring ExceptionHandler for handling the exception in a cleaner way without relying heavily on a programmatic approach.

This is true that it would have been safer to make this method static and independent from the instance, but at the same time it strongly implies that this logic does not belong in this class and should be provided during construction. This can be done using spring without losing the testable aspect of this class.

I second this, SR principle is broken, and properties have to be loaded during construction automatically by the class itself. 
This should be separated and provided.

If I'm not mistaken the enablePresets flag is not something that is changed during runtime. If the value won't change after construction, a better design approach would inject the final result during construction into this bean, instead of providing a switch for making the decision. There is no need to have a switch flag at this level because it suggests that something can change in the flow dynamically, which is untrue. Don't provide conditions for a flow, just provide the flow. It's crucial to make the decisions as quickly as possible if we can.

It is bad practice but invoking static methods isn't also a good approach. If something is static it is not part of the class instance, so it may be a good candidate for extraction in proper Dependency Injection. My suggestion, avoid any kind of method invocation (instance nor static) inside a constructor. Use a constructor to provide dependencies only. 
Use block initializers for logic (either instance, which or run after super() or static blocks). They are underused for such purpose and would be better suited for i.e. log configuration.
license header missing

- add static
- reconsider uppercase - is the logger really a constant? (see my previous comment)

hmm loadPresets is a little misleading here isn't it? :)
It does loadPresets from the files, but also registers them in the simulator. Kind of tricky and not quite clear from the API(public method) standpoint. Please reconsider a slightly different name :)
While there is nothing wrong with this class, why not simply use a pure java approach using getResource ? This way we could eliminate an unnecessary Spring import. Just a suggestion :)

can we change the logic a little bit so that we can have one point leading to where the simulator registration occurs? This can be wrapped in a simple private method call. It is important because a simulator is an outside dependency that is called the same way multiple times.
Shouldn't this file be under the wiremock resource subdir ?
license header missing

If you have a util class, there usually is one approach to using it - through the static methods it provides. There is no need for relying on instances here.
Do the following:
- make it final
- add a private constructor so that static members are not accessed through references if someone creates an object by mistake
Agree if you needed to add a comment to explain what the method does, just rename the method so that it better expresses the functionality

I think the port and simulator instance can be defined in the field
Suggestion - always use static imports, there is no added benefit from writing the class each time. This is especially important in case of many Asserts, because it affects the readability.
license header missing
Sorry, static, not private.
I think mock must be created per test. It will matter when you have more than 1 test.

Actually, they way junit works is it creates a new instance of the class before running each @Test. So from a JUnit perspective this code and approach is completely fine, no state is shared between the tests (unless you use static).

Component class shouldn't be instantiated more than once
You should never have logic in constructor. In this case - you're implicitly passing reference to this to other class, while still being in constructor. Change this to fabrication method.
Passing boolean to function (or constructor) usually means that function (or class) does two things. In this case - your class either do something or nothing.
You're calling "getMappingSource(filesRoot)" twice. Also you have logic in contructor.
Since guava 21.0 com.google.common.base.Predicate is functional interface and then you can omit "::apply".

Why don't you pass first exception as cause? You're loosing previous stack trace.

EDIT: this is framework exception. I think you can still log original exception to allow easier debugging.
Check if correct port is returned. I think it's better to check if correct port is returned, you shouldn't care if method .port() was called.
rename to something like urlCaptor
No need for checking size if you're checking content next line.

NullPointerException is signal that something in application went wrong - it's good practice to never catch it, but prevent from throwing it.

Also I would not throw NullPointerException when file is missing.

How I can prevent case "resource doesn't exists"?

I need to access directory which (in worst case) may not exist. See ClassLoader#getResource, it returns URL or null. No way to check if it exists.

It is literally NPE, it shouldn't happen, it went wrong.

But I am open to yours suggestions.

Definetly not NullPointeException. Specification says when NPE should be used: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/lang/NullPointerException.html

I'd suggest custom exception for this case.
This will not be called, because line above throws exception. If you want to check something else after exception is thrown you can use, for example, assertThatThrownBy from AssertJ.
